1: Chapter 9 begins the 'First Burden' (massa, H4853 = oracle, burden) of Zechariah — the first of two oracles (chs 9-11 and 12-14) that are structurally distinct from the dated night-vision section (chs 1-8). The undated character and the different style of chs 9-14 ('Deutero-Zechariah') have led many scholars to posit different authorship, though the unity of the book remains defensible. The First Burden opens with a geo-political sweep from north (Hadrach, Damascus) to south (Philistia), pronouncing judgment on the nations surrounding Israel — similar to the oracles-against-the-nations in Amos 1-2 and Jeremiah 46-51.
2: 'And Hamath also, which borders on it, Tyre and Sidon, though they are very wise.' The northern nations (Hamath on the Orontes, the commercial metropolises of Tyre and Sidon) are addressed. Tyre and Sidon were renowned for their wisdom in ancient tradition (Ezek 28:3-5: your wisdom and your understanding) — their commercial acumen made them wealthy and proud. Their wisdom (chakkam meod) could not protect them from the divine judgment that the oracle pronounces.
3: 'Tyre has built herself a rampart and heaped up silver like dust, and fine gold like the mud of the streets.' Tyre's legendary impregnable island fortress (an engineering marvel of the ancient world) and her accumulated wealth are described. The comparison 'silver like dust' (keturar kesef) and 'gold like mud of the streets' echoes Job 27:16 (if he heaps up silver like dust) — the accumulation that will be swept away. Alexander the Great fulfilled this oracle in 332 BCE, destroying Tyre by building a causeway to the island fortress (the debris of the mainland city was cast into the sea — fulfilling Ezek 26:12).
4: 'But behold, the Lord will strip her of her possessions and strike down her power on the sea, and she will be devoured by fire.' The divine judgment on Tyre's wealth and naval power: vehorisha (will dispossess her), vahikka bayam cheilah (will strike her power/wealth in the sea), vahi be'esh teikhal (she will be devoured by fire). Alexander's siege and destruction of Tyre (332 BCE) fulfilled this precisely: the city was besieged, its wealth was captured, and much of it was burned. The prophetic word authenticated by specific historical fulfillment was a pillar of OT apologetics (Deut 18:22).
5: 'Ashkelon shall see it and be afraid; Gaza too, and shall writhe in anguish; Ekron also, because its hopes are confounded. The king shall perish from Gaza; Ashkelon shall be uninhabited.' The Philistine cities witnessing Tyre's destruction will be paralyzed with fear — and then face their own judgment. The four Philistine cities (Ashkelon, Gaza, Ekron, Ashdod in v6) were the same targets as Zeph 2:4-7. Gaza (Azzah) losing her king echoes the wordplay of Zeph 2:4 (Azzah azuva = Gaza forsaken).
6: 'A mixed people shall dwell in Ashdod, and I will cut off the pride of Philistia.' Mamzer (H4464 — a person of mixed/uncertain ancestry, translated 'mixed people' or 'bastard') settling in Ashdod signals the dissolution of Philistine ethnic identity. The 'pride of Philistia' (ga'on plishtim) — the cultural, military, and political achievements that made the Philistines proud — will be cut off. The Philistine pentapolis as a coherent political entity effectively disappeared after the Hellenistic period.
7: 'I will take away its blood from its mouth, and its abominations from between its teeth; it too shall be a remnant for our God; it shall be like a clan in Judah, and Ekron shall be like the Jebusites.' The astonishing promise: after judgment, a Philistine remnant will join the people of God. 'Taking blood from its mouth' (removing the abomination of blood-eating, forbidden in the Mosaic law, Lev 17:14) represents purification. The Jebusite comparison is the most powerful: the Jebusites, inhabitants of Jerusalem before David (2 Sam 5:6-8), were absorbed into Israel after conquest. Similarly, the purified Philistine remnant will be incorporated into God's people — an early anticipation of Gentile inclusion.
8: 'Then I will encamp at my house as a guard, so that none shall march to and fro; no oppressor shall again march over them, for now I see with my own eyes.' YHWH promises to guard his house (the temple/land) personally — he himself as the sentry. 'No oppressor shall again march over them' — after the sequence of Assyrian, Babylonian, and potentially Greek subjugation, YHWH promises an end to foreign military transit through the land. 'For now I see with my own eyes' (ki attah ra'iti ve'einai) — YHWH's personal oversight is the guarantee. The verse connects the geo-political survey (vv1-7) to the coming salvation (vv9-10).
9: 'Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion! Shout aloud, O daughter of Jerusalem! Behold, your king is coming to you; righteous and having salvation is he, humble and mounted on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.' Zechariah 9:9 is one of the most precisely fulfilled messianic prophecies in the OT: Matthew (21:5) and John (12:15) both cite it explicitly at the Triumphal Entry. The verse is the theological center of the First Burden. Key terms: (1) tzaddik (righteous) — the king is himself righteous, not merely a just ruler; (2) nosha (having salvation, H3467 — either 'victorious' or 'saved by God' — the passive form means 'one who has been saved/vindicated by YHWH') — the king's victory is YHWH's work; (3) ani (humble, H6035 — poor, meek, afflicted — the same word used for the aniyim remnant of Zeph 3:12); (4) rochev al chamor (riding on a donkey) — a king on a donkey signals peace, not war (contrast a war-horse, v10 = the war-horse cut off).
10: 'I will cut off the chariot from Ephraim and the war horse from Jerusalem; and the battle bow shall be cut off, and he shall speak peace to the nations; his dominion shall be from sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth.' The messianic king brings universal peace: military instruments removed (chariot, horse, bow), peace proclaimed to the nations, dominion extending to the whole earth. The 'from sea to sea and from the River to the ends of the earth' is the covenant land promise (Gen 15:18; Ps 72:8) expanded to its maximum eschatological extent. The NT: Christ's command to lay down the sword (Matt 26:52), his proclamation of peace (John 14:27; Eph 2:14-17), and his coming universal rule (Rev 11:15).
11: 'As for you also, because of the blood of my covenant with you, I will set your prisoners free from the waterless pit.' The 'blood of your covenant' (bedam beritekh) — the Sinaitic covenant ratified by blood (Exod 24:8: behold the blood of the covenant). Because of the covenant's blood-bond, YHWH commits to liberating the prisoners. 'The waterless pit' (bor ein mavim) echoes Joseph's pit (Gen 37:24: the pit was empty, there was no water in it) and Jeremiah's pit-prison (Jer 38:6). The liberation from the pit is both the historical return from exile and the eschatological liberation that the NT applies to Christ's descent and resurrection (cf. 1 Pet 3:19).
12: 'Return to your stronghold, O prisoners of hope; today I declare that I will restore to you double.' The call to 'prisoners of hope' (asirei hatiqvah) — those who are imprisoned but hope for release — to return to the betzaron (stronghold, fortified place). The promise of double (mishneh) restoration echoes Job 42:10 (YHWH gave Job twice as much) and Isa 61:7 (instead of your shame you shall have a double portion). The double-restoration formula is the OT's overcorrection pattern: YHWH gives back more than was taken.
13: 'For I have bent Judah as my bow; I have made Ephraim its arrow. I will stir up your sons, O Zion, against your sons, O Greece, and wield you like a warrior's sword.' YHWH as the divine archer bending Judah as his bow and Ephraim as his arrow — the reunited nation (both Judah and Ephraim/northern Israel) is YHWH's weapon. The specific mention of Yavan (H3120, Greece/Javan) is remarkable: the sons of Zion versus the sons of Greece anticipates the Maccabean conflict (167-164 BCE), when Jewish resistance to Hellenization under Antiochus IV (the Maccabean revolt) appeared to be YHWH's people fighting YHWH's battle against Greek cultural imperialism.
14: 'Then the LORD will appear over them, and his arrow will go out like lightning; the Lord GOD will sound the trumpet and will march forth in the whirlwinds of the south.' YHWH as the Divine Warrior leading the battle: his arrow like lightning (chetz kevaraz yetze), the divine trumpet sounding (yitqah beshofar), the march through the storm-wind. The divine warrior theophany vocabulary (lightning, thunder, whirlwind, trumpet) echoes the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:16; 20:18) and the Hab 3 theophanic psalm. YHWH fights for his people.
15: 'The LORD of hosts will protect them, and they shall devour, and tread down the sling stones, and they shall drink and roar as if drunk with wine, and be full like a bowl, drenched like the corners of the altar.' The victory is YHWH's protection and the people's empowered action together. The devouring and treading down the sling stones suggests the vulnerability of the enemy's weapons; the drinking-and-roaring image is the intoxication of victory. The altar-corners analogy (kezaviyot hamizbeach) refers to the blood of sacrifices thrown against the altar corners — the people, through battle, become a kind of living sacrifice to YHWH's purposes.
16: 'On that day the LORD their God will save them, as the flock of his people; for like the jewels of a crown they shall shine on his land.' The reversal from battle-imagery to pastoral-imagery: those who just devoured enemies are now 'the flock of his people' (tzon ammo) saved by the Good Shepherd. 'Jewels of a crown' (avnei nezer) — precious stones of a diadem, sparkling over the land. The people who were scattered (1:19, 2:6) and imprisoned (9:11) are now jewels adorning YHWH's land.
17: 'For how great is his goodness, and how great his beauty! Grain shall make the young men flourish, and new wine the young women.' The closing doxology: YHWH's goodness (tuvo) and beauty (yafyo) — covenant blessing expressed aesthetically. The covenant prosperity (grain and new wine) specifically blessing the young (young men and young women) is the reversal of Joel 1:10-12 where grain and new wine were destroyed and the young mourned. The restoration of the young generation's blessing is the restoration of future hope.
